world.title: Top Canadian commander in Latvia temporarily removed following compensation probe
world.description: Canada's military operations commander has temporarily removed the officer in charge of the country's biggest overseas deployment. The decision by Lt.-Gen. Steve Boivin comes after Col. James Smith was charged by military police with prejudice to good order regarding compensation irregularities while he commanded Task Force Latvia.

business.description: Regular subway service has resumed on Line 2 in time for the afternoon rush hour following a lengthy closure on Tuesday as a result of a hydraulic oil spill on the tracks overnight.

science.title: A breath test reveals infections deep inside tissues
science.description: Bacteria turn sugars labeled with carbon-13 into detectable carbon dioxide, quickly identifying infections in mice

science.title: Intelligent monitoring pipe detects and predicts 3D soil settlement
science.description: An intelligent monitoring pipe combines optical sensing with machine learning algorithms to monitor and predict 3D soil settlement, which could help provide early warnings of risks from soil settlement and prevent pipeline displacement and accidents from building cracks.

science.title: Metamaterial chains learn new shapes by sharing data hinge to hinge
science.description: In a new Nature Physics publication, University of Amsterdam researchers introduce human-made materials that spring to life. These 'metamaterials' don't just learn to change shape, but can autonomously adapt their shape-changing strategy, perform reflex actions and move around like living systems do.

science.description: "When I read the Bible, and I look at all of the amazing things that were done for us..."

science.title: Water on the moon? New study narrows down the mostly likely locations
science.description: Astronauts on the moon could mine ice for drinking water or to make rocket fuel. A new study pinpoints a few cold, dark craters where water likely accumulated over billions of years.

science.title: From decades-long studies of humble grasses, new clues to climate resistance
science.description: A new analysis of nearly 40 years of data from three tracts of North American grassland confirms what researchers have long said: that biodiversity can be a natural defense against climate threats. But the study also reveals that coping with climate extremes isn’t just a numbers game where the more species an ecosystem has, the better. Multiple dimensions of biodiversity can help nature survive — and thrive — in harsh conditions, the researchers report.
